skill.name: ai-runtime-toolkit
skill.description: Toolkit for discovering, documenting, and running AI Runtime helper tools across Bash, Python, Node.js, and external CLI utilities.
skill.summary: Discover and manage AI Runtime diagnostic, code analysis, API testing, and external CLI tools.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and environment access patterns. Review confirmed legitimate toolkit behavior, but also confirmed risky patterns such as pipe-to-shell install guidance, local script execution, .env sourcing, broad filesystem scans, and API calls; no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection was found.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Pipe-to-Shell Installation Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill includes multiple instructions that download remote install scripts and pipe them directly to sh. This is a true positive because it trains users or agents to execute unaudited network content as shell code.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell commands are present in configuration and documentation. Legitimate installation intent exists, but the execution pattern is inherently dangerous.

high_findings.title: Environment File Sourcing Enables Shell Execution
high_findings.description: The service health checker sources a local .env file before reading database settings. This is risky because shell source executes file contents, not just key-value parsing, if the workspace .env is attacker controlled.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses source .env. The threat depends on local file control, but the execution behavior is clear.

high_findings.title: Documentation Encourages Executing Shell History
high_findings.description: The external tools guide shows a pipeline that lets a user select a command from shell history and execute it with bash. This can replay destructive or sensitive commands by mistake.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented command explicitly pipes selected history into bash. It is an example rather than auto-run code, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: Discovered Local Tool Execution
medium_findings.description: The CLI run command builds a command from a discovered tool file and passes user arguments to subprocess.run. This is intended toolkit behavior, but it means publishing the skill exposes local script execution paths that need trust and review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct and does not use shell=True. Risk remains because discovered scripts are executed with user-supplied arguments.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Diagnostics
medium_findings.description: The Bash tools read arbitrary log files, inspect disk paths, and enumerate directories. This is legitimate diagnostics behavior, but it can expose sensitive files or cause expensive scans on broad paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly operate on user-selected filesystem paths. No exfiltration was found, but local disclosure and performance risks are real.

medium_findings.title: Network Requests and Local Report Writes
medium_findings.description: The API tester sends requests to a base URL from arguments or API_BASE_URL and writes a report file. This is expected for API testing, but it needs clear user consent for external endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network and file-write behavior are explicit. The default localhost target lowers risk, while configurable targets keep the risk meaningful.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Static Hits Appear False Positive
low_findings.description: The static LLM role-token findings point to database user fields, not instructions to the evaluator or agent. No evidence found for text telling the model to ignore instructions, change risk levels, or skip analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is DB_USER metadata and a Bash default variable. It is not an LLM role token or instruction.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Appear False Positive
low_findings.description: Sampled weak-cryptography locations are metadata, model fields, documentation, or formatting code rather than cryptographic operations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak algorithms being used for security.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited context contains descriptive metadata and documentation. I did not find security-sensitive cryptographic use at those locations.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Static Hits Appear Documentation Related
low_findings.description: The high entropy findings are on Markdown and YAML files with Chinese text, tables, and examples. No evidence found of packed binaries, encrypted payloads, or obfuscated code in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are readable documentation or metadata. Entropy is likely inflated by non-English prose and structured examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: source .env in Shell Script
dangerous_patterns.description: Sourcing .env executes shell syntax and can run commands if the file is malicious. Use a strict dotenv parser or require explicit environment variables instead.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is a direct Bash source call. The impact depends on who controls .env.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote install scripts are piped directly into sh in multiple guides. This bypasses review and checksum verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell syntax is explicit and is a known high-risk installation pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Execution of Toolkit Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The discovery CLI executes a selected local tool file through subprocess.run. The implementation avoids shell=True, but it still runs local scripts from the package.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution path is clear. It is also the stated purpose of the toolkit, so the risk is contextual.

dangerous_patterns.title: Interactive Selection Piped to Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: A documented command sends selected shell history into bash. This can execute unintended or sensitive historical commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command explicitly ends with bash. It appears in documentation, not automatically executed code.

content.value_statement: Teams need one place to find and run AI Runtime helper tools. This skill organizes internal scripts and external CLI utilities for discovery, diagnostics, code analysis, and API testing.
actual_capabilities: Lists internal AI Runtime tools and external CLI tool definitions.
Searches and recommends tools by name, description, purpose, or category.
Runs discovered internal tool files through a command line interface.
Provides Bash utilities for service health checks, disk checks, and log analysis.
Includes Python utilities for dependency analysis and code statistics.
Includes a Node.js API tester that sends HTTP requests and exports a test report.
limitations: The toolkit executes local scripts and should only be used from trusted workspaces.
External CLI install instructions are guidance only and require separate security review.
Discovery logic is simple and depends on local metadata files and installed commands.
Diagnostics can read local files, scan directories, or contact configured endpoints.

use_cases.title: Audit Local Runtime Tools
use_cases.description: List available AI Runtime helpers, confirm external CLI availability, and identify which tool supports a specific operational task.

use_cases.title: Run Development Diagnostics
use_cases.description: Check service health, inspect logs, review disk status, and generate quick code or dependency reports during debugging.

use_cases.title: Document Tooling Standards
use_cases.description: Use the included references to explain approved CLI utilities, expected usage patterns, and onboarding steps for a team toolkit.

prompt_templates.title: List Available Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AI Runtime toolkit to list the available internal tools. Summarize what each tool does and which ones are safest to run first.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Diagnostic Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to investigate a failing local service. Search the toolkit for relevant health, log, and disk tools, then recommend a safe order of use.

prompt_templates.title: Review External CLI Dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the external CLI tools documented by this toolkit. Identify which are already available, which need installation, and any risky install commands.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Runtime Safety Before Execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Before running any toolkit command, inspect the target tool, explain its filesystem, network, and environment access, and ask for confirmation for risky actions.

output_examples.input: Find tools for investigating a slow API service.
output: Recommended service health checker for HTTP status and latency.
Recommended log analyzer for error and timeout patterns.
Recommended API tester for repeatable endpoint checks.

output_examples.input: Review the external CLI integration guidance.
output: Found recommended tools for search, navigation, JSON processing, and API testing.
Flagged pipe-to-shell install examples for manual review.

output_examples.input: Summarize code analysis options in the toolkit.
output_examples.output: The toolkit can produce dependency reports and code statistics. It reads local project files and can write reports, so it should run only on trusted paths.

best_practices: Inspect a tool file before using the run command, especially in community copies of the toolkit.
Use package managers or verified release checksums instead of pipe-to-shell install snippets.
Run diagnostics on the narrowest path or endpoint needed for the task.
anti_patterns: Do not source untrusted .env files or workspace scripts before review.
Do not run external installer commands from documentation without verifying the source.
Do not point API tests or filesystem scans at sensitive production targets by default.

faq.question: Does this skill install external CLI tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents external tools and can detect them, but installation remains a separate user action.

faq.question: Can it run commands on my machine?
faq.answer: Yes. The toolkit includes a run command for internal tool files and several executable scripts.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: No. Use it only in trusted workspaces because it can read files, run scripts, and contact endpoints.

faq.question: Does it send data to third-party services by default?
faq.answer: No third-party exfiltration was confirmed. The API tester can contact any base URL supplied by the user or environment.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level high?
faq.answer: The skill includes executable scripts, subprocess execution, environment access, filesystem scans, network calls, and unsafe install examples.

faq.question: What should reviewers check before publishing?
faq.answer: Review pipe-to-shell instructions, replace source .env parsing, add execution warnings, and document required user confirmations.
